structured_choices.data_source: Electronic health records from a single regional hospital spanning 36 months (Jan 2022–Dec 2024). Cohort: all patients with a primary or secondary diagnosis of heart failure (ICD-10 codes I50.x) at discharge. Inclusion: age ≥18, complete records for all required features. Exclusion: patients with <2 prior visits in the lookback window, incomplete outcome labels, or transfers to other facilities (outcomes not tracked). Expected N ≈ 5,000 patients, ≈ 6,500–7,000 hospitalizations (accounting for readmissions).
structured_choices.label_definition: Readmission (binary): unplanned hospital readmission within 30 days of discharge for any cause. Length of stay (continuous): calendar days from admission to discharge, measured for the index hospitalization. Both outcomes are extracted from structured discharge summaries and admission/discharge timestamps. Outcome observation window: outcomes are observed and locked at discharge; no post-discharge data leakage.
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal split by discharge date, stratified by severity at admission (using NYHA functional class or ejection fraction quartile) to ensure outcome distribution is representative across model types. Train: discharges Jan 2022–Aug 2023 (n ≈ 3,500). Validation: discharges Sep 2023–Nov 2023 (n ≈ 800). Test: discharges Dec 2023–Dec 2024 (n ≈ 1,700). Temporal split is appropriate because: (1) it reflects realistic deployment—the model is trained on historical data and evaluated on future, unseen patients; (2) it prevents data leakage from future patient trajectories; (3) it tests generalization over time, accounting for potential drift in hospital protocols or patient populations. Stratification within each fold ensures both readmission and LoS distributions are balanced, mitigating outcome imbalance issues.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (scalers, encoders, imputation strategies) are fit on the training set only and applied identically to validation and test sets. Feature engineering (e.g., derived vitals, lab trend calculations) is defined using train-only statistics. This prevents leakage of test-set information into the preprocessing pipeline. Missing values in vital signs and labs are imputed using mean/median within the training set; categorical missingness is flagged as a separate category. Any patient with >30% missing values across all features is excluded during preprocessing.
structured_choices.features: Baseline demographics: age, sex, BMI, smoking status, comorbidities (diabetes, hypertension, CKD, COPD—binary flags). Prior history: number of prior HF hospitalizations (in lookback window), prior ER visits (6 months), medication adherence proxy (pharmacy fill rate). Current admission vitals: systolic BP, diastolic BP, heart rate, respiratory rate, oxygen saturation, temperature (each at admission and as rolling 48-hour mean). Labs (at admission): BNP, troponin, creatinine, eGFR, hemoglobin, sodium, potassium. Derived features: ejection fraction (if available), LVEF category, decompensation severity score (composite of vitals + labs at admission). Temporal features: day of week admitted, seasonal indicator, time since last discharge (days). All continuous features are standardized (z-score) using train-set statistics. Categorical features are one-hot encoded. No features are derived from outcome (readmission or LoS); no discharge summary text is included in the initial feature set to avoid label leakage.
structured_choices.model: Multi-task learning model: a shared encoder (e.g., 2–3 dense hidden layers, 128–256 units, ReLU activation) processes the feature vector. Two task-specific heads branch from the shared representation: (1) a classification head for readmission (sigmoid output, 1 unit), and (2) a regression head for LoS (linear output, 1 unit). Loss is a weighted combination: L_total = α · L_readmission + (1 − α) · L_LoS, where α is a tuning hyperparameter (grid search over {0.3, 0.5, 0.7}). Architecture is identical across both models (single vs. multi-task comparison); only the loss function and head configuration differ.
structured_choices.baseline: Separate model approach: two independently trained models—(1) logistic regression for readmission, (2) linear regression for LoS—each with identical feature sets and preprocessing. Both baseline models are regularized (L2, tuned via grid search on validation data over λ in {0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1.0}). The baseline represents the standard practice; it is fair because it receives equivalent feature engineering, tuning effort (hyperparameter search on the same validation set), and is evaluated under identical conditions. Additionally, a naive baseline is included: predicting the empirical mean readmission rate and LoS from the training set, applied to all test instances—this establishes the floor.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Composite operational metric: (Readmission AUC-ROC × 0.6) + (LoS RMSE rank × 0.4). Rationale: (1) Readmission prevention is the primary clinical goal; AUC-ROC captures the model's ability to rank-order patients by risk across operating points, aligning with triage workflows. (2) LoS RMSE captures prediction accuracy for resource planning; lower RMSE indicates better operational efficiency. The 60/40 weighting reflects stakeholder priority (readmission reduction > cost optimization, though both matter). This composite metric directly maps to stated objectives: reducing readmissions and optimizing resource allocation. Alternative scoring: if calibration is critical for clinical decision-making, probability calibration error (ECE) is computed and reported separately.
secondary_metrics: Readmission: precision, recall, F1 at threshold optimized on validation data; per-subgroup performance (age <65 vs. ≥65, male vs. female, severity quartiles) to detect disparities
LoS: mean absolute error (MAE), median absolute error (MedAE) for robustness to outliers
Task correlation: Spearman correlation between predicted readmission risk and predicted LoS to assess whether shared representation captures clinically coherent structure
Calibration: expected calibration error (ECE) for readmission predictions; Sharpness-Calibration plot
Fairness: readmission AUC-ROC and LoS RMSE stratified by race/ethnicity (if available in EHR), hospital unit, physician team to detect confounding by care pathway
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Hyperparameters (learning rate, regularization, task weight α) are tuned via grid search over the validation set (Sep–Nov 2023). The model checkpoint with the highest composite metric on validation data is selected. Early stopping is applied during training if validation loss does not improve for 20 epochs. The test set (Dec 2023–Dec 2024) is reserved for final evaluation only; it is never accessed during tuning.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set is evaluated exactly once, after model selection and hyperparameter tuning are locked. Results are reported with 95% confidence intervals (via bootstrap resampling of test predictions, 1,000 replicates). No post-hoc tuning, threshold adjustment, or metric cherry-picking occurs on the test set. If test performance differs markedly from validation, a post-hoc analysis documents potential distribution shift (e.g., comparing cohort demographics, lab value distributions, readmission baseline rate between validation and test).
structured_choices.confound_controls: Physician/protocol heterogeneity: (1) stratification by hospital unit and admitting physician team during train/val/test splits ensures each fold contains similar distributions of care pathways; (2) secondary analysis re-runs the comparison within single high-volume physician or unit to isolate patient factors from provider effects; (3) logistic regression coefficients are inspected to verify that treatment intensity proxies (e.g., medication count, ICU admission) are not heavily weighted (suggesting the model is not simply learning institutional practice variation). Patient heterogeneity / generalization: (1) subgroup analysis reports performance by severity quintile, age group, comorbidity profile, and insurance status; (2) if performance degrades in a subgroup, this is documented as a limitation and flagged for prospective validation; (3) temporal drift analysis compares train and test cohort characteristics (t-tests on continuous variables, chi-square on categorical) to assess whether the patient population or outcomes baseline shifted over time. Outcome measurement: readmission status is verified in the EHR; any patients lost to follow-up are flagged and excluded. LoS is computed from structured timestamp fields, not manual review, to ensure consistency. All exclusion and data quality decisions are pre-specified; no data is removed post-hoc based on results.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: Multi-Task Learning for Heart Failure Readmission and Length-of-Stay Prediction

### 1. Data Collection and Cohort Definition

The study uses electronic health records from the regional hospital spanning 36 months (January 2022–December 2024). The cohort comprises all patients with a heart failure diagnosis code (ICD-10 I50.x) recorded at hospital discharge, age ≥18 years, with complete records for all required features. Patients with fewer than 2 prior visits in the lookback window or incomplete outcome labels are excluded. The expected sample size is approximately 5,000 patients, encompassing 6,500–7,000 index hospitalizations (accounting for readmissions). This dataset is sufficiently large to support both multi-task and baseline models and permits robust subgroup analysis.

### 2. Label and Outcome Definition

Readmission is defined as an unplanned hospital readmission within 30 days of discharge for any cause (binary outcome). Length of stay (LoS) is defined as the calendar number of days from admission to discharge for the index hospitalization (continuous outcome). Both outcomes are extracted from structured EHR fields and locked at discharge; no post-discharge follow-up data is used, preventing label leakage. Outcome observation is complete for all patients in the cohort by the end of the study window.

### 3. Data Split and Temporal Strategy

The data is split temporally by discharge date: training (Jan 2022–Aug 2023, n ≈ 3,500), validation (Sep 2023–Nov 2023, n ≈ 800), and test (Dec 2023–Dec 2024, n ≈ 1,700). Within each fold, records are stratified by admission severity (NYHA functional class or ejection fraction quartile) to ensure balanced representation of outcome distributions. This temporal split is essential because it mirrors real-world deployment—the model is trained on historical data and prospectively evaluated on future, unseen patients. It eliminates future information leakage and tests generalization over time. Stratification ensures that both readmission rates and LoS distributions are representative in each fold, mitigating outcome imbalance and enabling fair comparison between model architectures.

### 4. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Features are derived from four categories: (1) baseline demographics (age, sex, BMI, smoking status, comorbidities as binary flags), (2) prior history (number of prior HF hospitalizations, prior ER visits, medication adherence proxy), (3) admission vitals and labs (BP, HR, RR, SpO₂, temperature, BNP, troponin, creatinine, eGFR, hemoglobin, electrolytes), and (4) temporal context (day of week, seasonal indicator, time since last discharge). Derived features include ejection fraction category and a decompensation severity composite score. All continuous features are standardized (z-score normalization) using statistics computed from the training set only; this standardizer is then applied to validation and test sets. Categorical features are one-hot encoded. Missing values are imputed using training-set means/medians; missingness >30% triggers patient exclusion. Critically, no features are computed from the outcome variables, and discharge summary text is not included in the initial feature set to avoid label leakage.

### 5. Model Architecture and Baseline Comparison

The multi-task model consists of a shared encoder (2–3 dense layers, 128–256 units, ReLU activation) feeding into two task-specific heads: a classification head for readmission (sigmoid, 1 output) and a regression head for LoS (linear, 1 output). The loss function is a weighted combination: L_total = α · L_readmission + (1 − α) · L_LoS, where α is tuned via grid search (α ∈ {0.3, 0.5, 0.7}) on the validation set.

The baseline comprises two independently trained models: logistic regression for readmission and linear regression for LoS, each using the identical feature set and preprocessing. Both are regularized (L2, tuned over λ ∈ {0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1.0} on validation data). A naive baseline predicting empirical mean readmission rate and LoS serves as the floor. The separate-model baseline is fair because it receives equivalent feature engineering, hyperparameter tuning effort, and evaluation conditions.

### 6. Model Selection and Validation

Hyperparameter tuning occurs exclusively on the validation set. Early stopping is applied if validation loss stagnates for 20 consecutive epochs. The model checkpoint achieving the highest composite metric on validation data is locked and evaluated once on the test set. The test set is never accessed during hyperparameter search.

### 7. Primary and Secondary Metrics

The primary metric is a composite: (Readmission AUC-ROC × 0.6) + (LoS RMSE rank × 0.4). This weighting reflects the clinical priority: preventing readmissions (primary goal) while optimizing resource use (secondary goal). AUC-ROC captures ranking ability across operating points; RMSE captures absolute prediction error for resource planning. Secondary metrics include readmission precision/recall/F1, LoS MAE, per-subgroup performance (age, sex, severity quartile, race/ethnicity), and prediction calibration (expected calibration error for readmission probabilities). Fairness analysis stratifies results by patient demographics and care pathway (unit, physician) to detect confounding by institutional practice.

### 8. Controls for Confounds

Physician/protocol heterogeneity is addressed through stratification by hospital unit and admitting team during train/val/test splits. Post-hoc subgroup analysis isolates patient-level effects from provider effects. Patient heterogeneity is examined via stratified performance reporting. Temporal drift is assessed by comparing cohort characteristics and outcome baselines between train and test. All exclusion criteria and preprocessing decisions are pre-specified; no post-hoc data removal occurs based on results.

### 9. Scope of Inference

This design establishes whether multi-task learning outperforms separate models for heart failure readmission and LoS prediction **at this regional hospital, for this patient population, within this time window**. The temporal split does not establish causality or clinical efficacy of the predictions—only comparative model performance. Prospective clinical validation and provider workflow integration studies are separate, necessary steps before deployment.
